title: System Load

content.statement: Every system has a breaking point, overload creates collapse.
content.question: Is your current load above 0.7? What do you cut THIS week?
content.life_lens: Running business + home + cooking + fitness = collapse risk.
sop: List every current responsibility, client, or commitment pulling on your time and capacity.
Estimate your Demand (count of active commitments) and your sustainable Capacity (see calculation, page 4)
Calculate your Load Ratio, Demand divided by Capacity (see calculation, page 4)
If it's above 0.7, cut or delegate at least one item this week.
Build a buffer of at least 20% unused capacity so one surprise doesn't collapse the whole system.
questions: Is your current load above 0.7? What do you cut THIS week?
What's the first thing that breaks when you're overloaded, and has it already started breaking quietly?
If you added one more commitment today, what would you have to drop to keep the system from collapsing?
content.equation_example: Demand = 8 (count of active commitments), Capacity = 10 (count you can sustainably handle). Load Ratio = 8 ÷ 10 = 0.8. Above 0.7 means you're in overload territory, quality and consistency start breaking down. Below 0.7 means you have buffer to absorb shocks without collapse.
content.closing_statement: Untested capacity isn't capacity you can rely on.

caption:
Principle Nº 130: System Load

Every system has a breaking point, overload creates collapse.

Ask yourself: Is your current load above 0.7? What do you cut THIS week?
